points: Nationalism in India grew as an anti-colonial response to post-WWI economic hardships (taxes, prices, forced recruitment) and common oppression under British rule.
Mahatma Gandhi introduced Satyagraha—a mass agitation based on truth and non-violence—starting with successful local interventions in Champaran, Kheda, and Ahmedabad.
The repressive Rowlatt Act and the tragic Jallianwalla Bagh massacre (1919) converted local protests into a nationwide struggle, prompting the merger of nationalist and Khilafat issues.
The Non-Cooperation Movement (1921-22) focused on boycotting British institutions and goods to collapse colonial rule through non-participation, but was withdrawn after the Chauri Chaura violence.
The Civil Disobedience Movement (1930) began with the historic Salt March to Dandi, symbolically uniting the nation by challenging the government's monopoly on an essential commodity.
While Non-Cooperation emphasized non-participation, Civil Disobedience called for active violation of colonial laws, culminating in the final peak of the struggle—the 1942 Quit India Movement.

Diverse social groups including rich/poor peasants, business classes, and industrial workers participated with varied aspirations, sometimes leading to internal conflicts over specific demands like rent remission or wages.
The movement faced limits concerning Dalit representation (resolved through the Poona Pact) and growing communal alienation between Hindus and Muslims as the struggle evolved.

A sense of collective belonging was forged through cultural processes like the image of Bharat Mata, revival of Indian folklore, nationalist flags, and a prideful reinterpretation of India’s ancient history.
